summary: The core discovery routes, canonical wiring, and required commands are in place, but the sitemap `lastmod` contract is still substantively wrong for homepage removals. `src/features/public-site/discovery.ts` computes homepage `lastModified` only from currently published items, so after unpublishing a note or link the homepage entry can move backward to an older timestamp instead of reflecting the unpublish-time public change. That conflicts with `docs/product-specs/public-sitemap-and-seo.md`, which requires `lastmod` values to reflect meaningful public content change. The new E2E regression in `tests/e2e/seo-discovery.spec.ts` currently codifies that incorrect rollback behavior by expecting the homepage `lastmod` to return to its initial value after unpublish.
satisfied_exit_criteria: `/sitemap.xml` and `/robots.txt` exist and the sitemap enumerates only `/` plus published note detail URLs, excluding standalone link URLs.
Robots output advertises the sitemap when `SITE_URL` is configured and fails closed when it is missing/invalid via the shared public-origin helper.
Public home and note pages emit canonical URLs through the same shared origin contract.
`npm run test:e2e -- --grep @seo-discovery` passed in the provided deterministic check summary.
`npm run verify` passed in the provided deterministic check summary.
missing_requirements: Homepage sitemap `lastmod` does not correctly represent unpublish/removal events; it regresses to the newest remaining published item's timestamp instead of the time the public homepage actually changed.
Regression coverage should protect the correct homepage `lastmod` behavior for publish and unpublish mutations rather than asserting the rollback to the pre-publish timestamp.
human_review_triggers: The homepage sitemap entry changes after unpublish, but to an older timestamp; that is easy to miss unless someone inspects the actual `lastmod` semantics instead of just diffing URLs.
The current `@seo-discovery` test suite now blesses the backward-moving `lastmod`, so promotion would lock in the wrong contract.
